title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
permanent pasture producing Milk, cow, raw (FPCM) in New Zealand. Additional description: On-site, milk production (2017/2018). What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: The system boundary of the production system includes:

* All main processes of the system and its main production inputs
* The whole dairy herd, including lactating and dry cows, heifers, calves, and bulls
* Non-lactating animals of the dairy herd (e.g. bulls for insemination and labor, female calves and heifers for replacement, male calves and young bulls for sale)
* Production of local feeds, including fertilizers, irrigation, pesticides, and machinery
* Production of ancillary inputs such as fuels and electricity
* Drinking water for the cattle
* Farm infrastructure, including roads, irrigation infrastructure, buildings, and tools (excluding specialized tools)
* Manure and slurry
* On-farm consumption of electricity and fuels for animal feeding, milking, lighting, and cleaning
* Manure management, including N and CH4 emissions due to manure storage
* Manure application, including N and P field emissions from manure produced by animals reared at farms
* Production of on-farm feeds, including agricultural operations, production, and application of chemical fertilizers and pesticides
* Production of purchased feeds, including cultivation and processing
* Background data of purchased feed production, including CO2 emissions from land use change (if applicable)

title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
animal housing producing Meat, chicken (liveweight) in Canada. Additional description: A free-range system is defined as either a single- or multi-tier system (hens live either directly on the floor or within an aviary structure, with access to perches, nest and scratch areas, and other enrichments) in which hens are provided with outdoor access.. What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the relevant life cycle assessment (LCA) information:

**System Boundaries:**

* The system boundaries include all relevant material and energy inputs and emissions from the cradle-to-customer supply chains of Canadian egg and egg products in 2012.
* The system includes:
	+ Breeder flock facilities
	+ Feed production
	+ Water treatment
	+ Manure management
	+ Spent hen disposal
	+ Layer facilities
	+ Grading facilities
	+ Packaging process
	+ Transportation
	+ Slaughterhouse
	+ Broiler chicken production
* The system excludes:
	+ Vaccines
	+ Cleaning
	+ Animal feed production (except for feed used in layer facilities)
	+ Wastewater treatment (except for WWTP treatment in feed production)

**Housing Systems:**

* Conventional cage systems
* Enriched cage systems
* Free-range systems
* Free-range systems
* Organic systems

**Data Collection:**

* Survey was used to collect data from farmers regarding housing system type and characteristics, management practices, on-farm resource use, and hen performance.
* Survey responses were collected in two ways: in-person interviews with farm owners/managers and online surveys.

**

title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
permanent pasture producing Beef cattle, bull in Brazil. Additional description: Bull calves are fattened over a period of 20 months until they are a life weight of around 480 kg and sold to slaughter.. What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the relevant life cycle assessment (LCA) information:

**System boundary:** The system boundary includes the production of beef cattle from permanent pasture in Brazil, from birth to slaughter.

**Inputs:**

* Pasture production
* Mineral salt
* Electricity
* Fuels
* Fertilizers
* Water
* Transportation
* Breeding-rearing and fattening

**Outputs:**

* Beef cattle
* Manure
* Water

**Functional unit:** 1 kg live weight (LW) of beef cattle.

**Life cycle stages:**

1. Breeding and reproduction
2. Fattening (20 months)
3. Slaughter

**Assumptions:**

* Grazing takes place on low productive grassland yielding approximately 3000 kg dry matter per ha
* Conserved forage is produced on highly productive grassland yielding 8000 kg dry matter per ha
* Bull calves are separated from dairy cows soon after birth and reared artificially in the dairy herd until achieving a weight of approximately 55 kg
* Milk for family consumption replaces commercial milk from an international dairy company
* Milk for calves is assumed to replace a locally available milk substitute for calves.

context:

* Purchase of bedding materials (straw, corn stalks, sawdust)
* Purchase of productive agricultural factors required for on-farm feeds production (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides)
* Manure from animal breeding
* Production of ancillary inputs such as fuels and electricity
* Drinking water for the cattle

* Transport of animal feed and milk products
* Milk processing and packaging
* Retail and consumer level emissions and resource consumption

The system boundary does not include:

* Equipment of the farms (roads, irrigation infrastructure, buildings, tools)

* Manure management system (after the point of export)
* Major building materials for farm and processing plant buildings (amortized over 50 years for farm buildings and 30 years for milk plant buildings)
* Employee transport and corporate office operation
* Delivery of final products from the dairy factory to wholesale and retailers

* Climate change (CC)
* Eutrophication potential (EP)
* Acidification potential (AP)
* Land occupation (LO)
* Biodiversity damage (BD)
* Abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (AD)

**Excluded Processes:**

* Production of premix and fish meal
* Transport and slaughter of animals

title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
permanent pasture producing Beef cattle, cow in Brazil. Additional description: Beef, Stylosanthes spp. (cv. Campo Grande) pasture (Scenario 3). What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the relevant life cycle assessment (LCA) information:

**System Boundaries:**

* The system boundary includes the production of beef cattle from birth to slaughter, including:
	+ Extensive (EXT) and intensive (INT) beef production systems
	+ Grassland and pasture management
	+ Feed production and processing
	+ Manure management
	+ Energy use and emissions

**Subsystems:**

* Extensive (EXT) system:
	+ Grazing on native pasture with little or no supplementation
	+ No fencing used
	+ Cattle are raised as an asset or savings
* Intensive (INT) system:
	+ Three subsystems: cow-calf, backgrounding, and fattening
	+ Grazing and supplementation with mineral salts
	+ Use of purchased concentrate feeds and locally produced agricultural byproducts

**Functional Unit:**

* 1 kg of body weight gain (BWG) over the nursery stage

**Input and Output:**

* Inputs:
	+ Feed ingredients (cassava, palm kernel meal, rice bran, soybean meal, molasses, and urea)
	+ Mineral salts
	+ Fertilizers
	+ Energy (fuel, electricity, and heat)

context:
permanent pasture producing Sheep, lamb in United Kingdom. Additional description: High sugar and white clover pasture mix for sheep and cattle.. What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the relevant life cycle assessment (LCA) information:

**System boundary:** The system boundary includes the production of sheep, including feed grown on the farm and imported to the farm, and the production of crops and stubble. It excludes construction of infrastructure and farm establishment.

**Data sources:** Data were collected from three years of written farm records, business accounting systems, and literature and agricultural models such as APSIM.

**Case studies:** Three sheep production systems were modeled:

1. UK system: focused on meat production, with wool as a by-product.
2. New Zealand system: focused on wool production, with lamb and mutton as primary products.
3. Australian system: composite crossbreeding system, producing low-value wool suitable for interior textiles.

**System boundaries for each case study:**

* UK system: includes sheep rearing, feedlot phase, and production of crops and stubble.
* New Zealand system: includes sheep rearing, feedlot phase, and production of crops and stubble.
* Australian system: includes sheep rearing, feedlot phase, and production of crops and stubble.

**Inputs and outputs:**

* Sheep: feed, health treatments, shearing, lambing, and wool production.
* Crops

title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
producing Beef cattle, bull in France. Additional description: Annual cycle of an alternative cow-calf production system in the Charolais basin, in which cattle are fed wrapped grass silage, in order to increase their omega-3 fatty acid intake. This consists of three components: 1) Reproduction, output of which = weaned male calves, weaned female calves not used for replacement cull cows, cull cows and a breeding bull. 2) Rearing female calves from weaning (9 months) to finishing at 33 months. 3) Finishing of cull cows, i.e. fattened before sending to the slaughterhouse. Both St and O3 are classified as extensive systems with 7.5 months grazing from April to November. The grazed area consists of 88% permanent versus 12% temporary grassland - this has been used to calculate the relative time spent on permanent versus temporary grassland during those 7.5 months of grazing.. What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the life cycle assessment (LCA) relevant information:

**System Boundaries:**

* The system includes all stages from breeding to slaughter, including:
	+ Reproduction (birth, weaning, culling)
	+ Rearing (weaning to finishing)
	+ Finishing (fattening before slaughter)
* Impacts from transport of bulls to the slaughterhouse and processing of meat are excluded.
* The system includes:
	+ Feed production (cultivation, seeding, fertilizing, pesticide application, liming, and harvest)
	+ Breeding (recreational activity in the herd, care taking of cows, heifers, and calves)
	+ Fattening
	+ Transport of animals between farms
* Manure is handled as solid manure on some farms and liquid manure on others, but is reused for fertilization and does not constitute a by-product from the system.

**Input and Output:**

* Inputs:
	+ Feed (maize silage, beet pulp, concentrates)
	+ Fertilizers and pesticides
	+ Diesel and electricity
	+ Agricultural machinery
* Outputs:
	+ Beef cattle
	+ Manure
	+ Fattened cattle

title: System Boundary Completeness
context:
animal housing producing Layer chicken in Czech Republic. Additional description: Organic production system for egg production from laying hens. What is in the system boundary? Additional Context: Here is a summary of the life cycle assessment (LCA) relevant to the context:

**System Boundaries:**

* The production system includes:
	+ Chicken farm
	+ Slaughterhouse
	+ Feed production
	+ Vaccines and antibiotics (excluded due to lack of information)
	+ Cleaning activities (excluded due to lack of significant environmental impact)
	+ Wastewater treatment (included in feed production)
	+ Organic waste management (included in chicken farm)
	+ Packaging and distribution

**Excluded Processes:**

* Production of cleaning agents (detergents and disinfectants)
* Solid-stream processing (excluded due to valorization)
* Capital goods and infrastructures (excluded)
* Farm-related activities outside of the chicken farm and slaughterhouse (e.g. production of bedding materials, distribution to the farm)

**Included Processes:**

* Animal feed production
* Vaccines and antibiotics (used in the chicken farm)
* Cleaning activities (in the chicken farm)
* Wastewater treatment (in the feed production and chicken farm)
* Organic waste management (in the chicken farm)
* Packaging and distribution
* Slaughterhouse activities (killing, scalding, cleaning, and packaging)
